intro: The biggest real distinction between New Relic and Dynatrace is straightforward: New Relic uses hybrid pricing with $0.30/GB (Standard), $49/core user, a free tier, enterprise plan available; Dynatrace uses seat-based pricing at $7, a free tier, enterprise plan available. New Relic sits at hybrid pricing with $0.30/GB (Standard), $49/core user, a free tier, enterprise plan available and carries moderate lock-in, while Dynatrace sits at seat-based pricing at $7, a free tier, enterprise plan available and carries moderate lock-in. New Relic is a fit when you need microservices performance monitoring — typical scenarios include microservices performance monitoring. Dynatrace is aimed at microservices performance monitoring, with workloads like microservices performance monitoring as the common case. The honest trade-off: New Relic is explicitly not for simple, static projects; Dynatrace is not intended for simple, static projects. On cost specifically, pricing model matters more than sticker price — one workload can make New Relic cheaper while a different shape flips the answer to Dynatrace.
quick_take: New Relic is for microservices performance monitoring; Dynatrace is for microservices performance monitoring; decide based on cost model and budget.
verdict_a: Choose New Relic if your primary workload is microservices performance monitoring and hybrid pricing ($0.30/GB (Standard), $49/core user) is acceptable.
verdict_b: Choose Dynatrace if your primary workload is microservices performance monitoring and seat-based billing at $7 matches your team size.

faq.q: Is New Relic cheaper than Dynatrace?
faq.a: It depends on workload shape: New Relic lists $0.30/GB (Standard), $49/core user, while Dynatrace lists $7. With different pricing models (hybrid vs seat), the cheaper option flips based on seat count, usage volume, and whether you use the free tier. Do a per-workload estimate rather than comparing headline numbers.

faq.q: Can I migrate from New Relic to Dynatrace?
faq.a: Migrating from New Relic to Dynatrace is doable but not automatic — New Relic carries moderate lock-in. Since both tools sit in the same category (Performance Monitoring / APM), the conceptual mapping is closer, but you still need to validate feature parity against your specific workload. Plan migration as a project, not a script: inventory integrations, test on non-production workloads, and keep New Relic running in parallel until Dynatrace is verified.

Our data gives New Relic and Dynatrace the same developer experience score (5/5), so neither has a clear edge on DX by that measure. The differentiator is usually how each tool fits your workflow — things like CLI quality, docs, local dev loop, and error messages. The safest call is to pilot both on a representative task and see which one gets out of your way.

faq.q: Is Dynatrace a good alternative to New Relic?
faq.a: Dynatrace is in the same category as New Relic (Performance Monitoring / APM), so it's a reasonable alternative to evaluate. Both tools claim similar best-fit workloads on paper, so the real test is how each behaves under your specific constraints. Note that Dynatrace is explicitly not for simple, static projects — if that describes your workload, it's not a viable replacement.
